citation: Ruiz, J. T., Gaunaurd, I. A., Best, T. M., Feeley, D., Mann, J. B., & Feigenbaum, L. A. (2024). Multi-modal approach to mitigating hamstring injuries in Division I college football athletes. Encyclopedia, 4, 1482-1495.
abstract: This article describes a Division I college football hamstring injury mitigation model built around interdisciplinary daily review, GPS-directed load analysis, repeated performance testing, force plate monitoring, wearable IMU assessment, and flexible strength and conditioning interventions. The authors report that the program produced no game time lost from hamstring injury across three consecutive years in a large roster environment.
tldr: A practical hamstring program should combine load monitoring, limb asymmetry testing, targeted strength work, and rapid staff communication rather than relying on one screening score.
methods: Narrative/practice model from a Division I football program. The paper outlines use of GPS workload review, force plates, ReLOAD Sport IMU sleeves for single-limb stability and dynamic movement metrics, performance-based testing, rehabilitation decision making, and individualized strength/prehab programming across training cycles.
findings: The authors attribute three consecutive years with no game time loss from hamstring injury to a coordinated multi-modal system. Force plates and wearable kinematic tests were framed as complementary tools for identifying asymmetries or movement deficits, while GPS was used to manage exposure and sprint/change-of-direction demands.
limitations: This is not a controlled trial and cannot isolate which component caused the reported outcome. The program was delivered in a well-resourced Power Five-style environment, datasets were not publicly available because of privacy restrictions, and findings may not generalize directly to smaller staffs or different roster structures.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the value is the operating model: review sprint exposure and asymmetry flags together, connect sport science outputs to athletic training and strength decisions the same day, and intervene early when a player becomes an outlier rather than waiting for symptoms to escalate.
athleteDev: Use hamstring risk work as year-round athlete development: build eccentric strength, hip extension capacity, trunk/pelvis control, and sprint tolerance while adjusting exercises by training phase and player position.
rtp: For return to play, combine symptom resolution with objective limb-to-limb force, balance, agility, and workload progression data; a player should re-enter high-speed work only after staff agree that testing and GPS exposure support the next step.

citation: Bergstein, V. E., Shu, H. T., Bodendorfer, B. M., DeFroda, S., & Aiyer, A. A. (2023). Multiple ankle injuries are associated with an increased risk of subsequent concussion in National Football League players. Foot & Ankle Orthopaedics, 8(4), 1-6.
abstract: This retrospective NFL study used ProFootballReference.com injury records from the 2009-2010 through 2019-2020 seasons to test whether ankle injuries were associated with later concussion. Among 5538 players, 941 had an ankle injury, 633 had a concussion, and 240 had both an ankle injury and a subsequent concussion. A single ankle injury was not associated with later concussion, but multiple ankle injuries were associated with higher concussion odds after adjustment for BMI, age, and position.
tldr: Repeated ankle injury history may be a head-injury risk marker, likely through impaired balance, instability, or altered movement strategy.
methods: Retrospective comparative cohort using public NFL injury data. Multivariable logistic regression tested concussion risk after ankle injury while adjusting for BMI, age, player position, and offense/defense/special-teams designation; descriptive comparisons used t tests and chi-square testing.
findings: The adjusted odds ratio for concussion after a single ankle injury was 0.90 and not significant. The adjusted odds ratio after multiple ankle injuries was 2.87, indicating substantially higher subsequent concussion risk. Special teams players had lower concussion odds after ankle injury than offensive or defensive players.
limitations: The study shows association, not causation. Public injury reports did not provide exact ankle diagnosis, severity, laterality, prior pre-NFL injuries, playing surface, weather, detailed exposure, verified head/neck diagnoses excluded from concussion coding, or return-to-play/performance outcomes.
practicalImplications: Baylor should treat repeated ankle sprains as more than a local joint issue. Players with recurrent ankle history deserve balance, vestibular/visual-motor, landing, cutting, and contact-readiness checks before full exposure, especially at positions with frequent open-field collisions.
athleteDev: Long-term development should emphasize ankle stiffness, proprioception, foot/ankle strength, deceleration mechanics, and single-leg control so recurrent ankle problems do not become a broader movement and collision-risk profile.
rtp: Return-to-play decisions after recurrent ankle injury should include sport-specific balance under fatigue, reactive cutting, perturbation/contact tolerance, and confidence, not just pain-free running and bracing clearance.

citation: Routledge, H. E., Leckey, J. J., Lee, M. J., Garnham, A., Graham, S., Burgess, D., Burke, L. M., Erskine, R. M., Close, G. L., & Morton, J. P. (2018). Muscle glycogen utilization during an Australian Rules football game. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance.
abstract: This case study quantified vastus lateralis glycogen use in two elite male Australian Rules football forwards during an in-season match. After a 24-hour carbohydrate-loading protocol of 8 g/kg plus 2 g/kg in the pre-match meal, biopsies were taken before and after competition while GPS quantified external load. Player A consumed water only and showed a 66% glycogen decrease; player B consumed 88 g carbohydrate during play and showed a 24% decrease.
tldr: High-speed intermittent football can draw heavily on muscle glycogen; pregame carbohydrate availability and in-game fueling may protect late-game capacity.
methods: Two elite SANFL forwards completed a standardized carbohydrate-loading protocol before a competitive game. Muscle biopsies from vastus lateralis were obtained pre- and post-match, GPS tracked match load, and one player consumed carbohydrate gels while the other consumed only water.
findings: Both players began with high glycogen availability above 500 mmol/kg dry weight. Player A played 115 minutes, covered 12.2 km, and dropped from 656 to 223 mmol/kg dry weight. Player B played 98 minutes, covered 11.2 km, consumed 88 g carbohydrate, and dropped from 544 to 416 mmol/kg dry weight. The authors suggest Australian Rules match play may have a higher glycogen cost than soccer or rugby and that carbohydrate feeding may spare glycogen.
limitations: The sample was only two players from one position group in one match, so individual workload, fiber-type response, diet, training status, and in-game fueling effects cannot be separated. Biopsies were not taken during each quarter or immediately after warm-up.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the direct transfer is fueling high-speed, repeat-effort days. Skill players and high-volume athletes should not enter major practices, scrimmages, or games under-fueled; nutrition plans should match expected sprint volume, duration, heat stress, and recovery windows.
athleteDev: Teach athletes to link carbohydrate intake with training quality, not just body weight. Developing repeat sprint capacity requires enough fuel to support high-intensity work and enough recovery nutrition to replenish glycogen before the next demanding session.
rtp: For return to play from lower-body injury, avoid judging readiness from a short fresh trial only. Progress toward full sessions should include fueling, repeat high-speed bouts, and next-day recovery monitoring because glycogen depletion can mask or worsen movement quality late in exposure.

citation: Flores, H. N., Tamujo, A. C., Cetolin, T., Ribeiro-Alvares, J. B., Noronha, M. A., Baroni, B. M., & Haupenthal, A. (2024). Muscle pain code: A novel tool for screening the risk of time-loss muscle injury in professional male football players. The Physician and Sportsmedicine.
abstract: This four-season cohort study tested whether a post-match Muscle Pain Code (MPC) predicted subsequent time-loss muscle injury in professional male soccer players. The MPC classified second-post-match-day pain as code 0 no pain, code 1 generalized muscle pain, code 2 diffused site muscle pain, or code 3 specific site muscle pain. Across 80 players and 1656 analyzed cases, localized pain codes were strongly associated with time-loss muscle injury in the next five days.
tldr: Where pain is located may matter more than pain alone: specific or well-outlined muscle pain two days after competition should trigger staff review.
methods: Players who had at least 45 minutes in two consecutive matches within seven days were included as cases. Staff collected MPC ratings on the second post-match day and then tracked time-loss muscle injuries for the following five days across four seasons.
findings: There were 229 time-loss muscle injuries. Only about 2% of cases with no pain or generalized pain led to time-loss injury, while 63% of code 2 and 78% of code 3 cases were followed by time-loss injury. Relative to no pain, code 2 and code 3 carried substantially higher injury odds; code 1 did not.
limitations: The injured muscle group was not linked to each reported pain code, structural versus functional diagnoses were not available, some absence-duration data were lost, and workload, sleep, diet, hydration, and recovery strategies were not modeled. Some athletes also struggled to distinguish code 2 from code 3.
practicalImplications: Baylor can add a quick body-map style question to post-game and heavy-practice check-ins: generalized soreness may be acceptable, but focal or clearly bounded muscle pain should prompt evaluation, modified exposure, and communication between sport science, athletic training, and strength staff.
athleteDev: The MPC concept supports athlete education. Players should learn to report focal muscle pain early and accurately instead of framing all soreness as normal football soreness.
rtp: For RTP and reintegration, localized post-session muscle pain should slow progression even if performance metrics look acceptable. Use the next five days after hard exposure as a watch window for symptoms, load modification, and treatment response.

citation: Dengel, D. R., Studee, H. R., Juckett, W. T., Bosch, T. A., Carbuhn, A. F., Stanforth, P. R., & Evanoff, N. G. (2024). Muscle-to-bone ratio in NCAA Division I collegiate football players by position. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research.
abstract: This retrospective DXA study compared total and regional muscle-to-bone ratio (MBR) in 553 NCAA Division I college football players and 261 healthy age-matched controls, then examined positional differences. Football players had greater lean mass, fat mass, bone mineral content, and bone mineral density than controls, but total MBR did not differ significantly. Regional and positional MBR patterns varied across offensive linemen, defensive linemen, tight ends, linebackers, running backs, specialists, quarterbacks, defensive backs, and wide receivers.
tldr: DXA-derived muscle-to-bone ratio gives position-specific context for body composition changes, but it should complement performance data rather than define an ideal body type by itself.
methods: Previously collected preseason DXA scans from Division I football players were compared with age-, sex-, and BMI-matched college controls. Total and regional lean mass, fat mass, and bone mineral content were used to calculate MBR, with position-group comparisons analyzed statistically.
findings: Football players were taller, heavier, and had higher lean mass, fat mass, bone mineral content, and bone density than controls, while total MBR was similar. College players had lower trunk MBR than controls. Defensive and offensive linemen had higher total MBR than several skill groups, running backs were higher than defensive backs and wide receivers, and regional MBR differed meaningfully by position.
limitations: The study was cross-sectional and cannot show ideal developmental trajectories or injury/performance thresholds. Only athletes who fit entirely in the DXA scan field were included, scans came from multiple universities, timing and hydration/nutrition controls were imperfect, and position averages may not fit individual role demands.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, MBR can help interpret body composition changes during development, especially when athletes gain mass. Use DXA trends to ask whether added size is supported by bone and lean tissue, but anchor decisions to position role, movement quality, strength, speed, and durability.
athleteDev: Position-specific MBR norms can guide offseason plans: linemen, backs, and skill players may need different lean-mass targets and regional development priorities, but each athlete's optimal profile should be judged by performance output and health response.
rtp: MBR is not an acute RTP test, but DXA trends may inform longer-term clearance after bone stress, major weight change, or extended inactivity by showing whether lean mass and bone-related measures are returning toward the athlete's position and personal baseline.
